title: Basic Chat Loop
description: A simple chat loop with no tool usage. The agent receives a user message and responds with a plain text explanation. This is the foundation of all agent interactions.

steps.content: Can you explain recursion in Java with a simple example?
steps.annotation: The user sends a plain text message. This enters the agent's chat loop as a new conversation turn.
The LLM generates a response. No tool calls are needed — the model has enough knowledge to answer directly. The chat loop completes this turn.

steps.content: What about tail recursion? Does Java optimize it?
steps.annotation: A follow-up message starts a new iteration of the chat loop. The conversation history (including the previous exchange) is included in the context.
The agent answers using its training data. This is a pure knowledge response — no external tools or actions required. The chat loop continues waiting for the next user message.
The user signals satisfaction. The agent can close the conversation gracefully.

steps.content: You're welcome! To summarize the key takeaway: always prefer iteration over deep recursion in Java since the JVM doesn't optimize tail calls. If you do use recursion, make sure your base case is correct to avoid `StackOverflowError`. Happy coding!
steps.annotation: Final response. The chat loop ends naturally when no further user input arrives. This basic loop pattern — receive message, generate response, repeat — is the core of every agent.
